subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 14! In this unit you will grow from someone who can read a clock into someone who can truly manage time. You already know how to tell the time to the nearest five minutes. Now you will learn the 24-hour clock, work out how long things take, read real timetables for buses and school, use a calendar to count days and weeks, and solve clever time problems that come up in everyday life.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 3 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Read and write times using both the 12-hour clock (with a.m. and p.m.) and the 24-hour clock.
Convert a time from 12-hour to 24-hour format and back again.
Work out a time interval (how long something lasts) between a start time and an end time.
Find an end time when you know the start time and how long something takes.
Find a start time when you know the end time and how long something takes.
Read and answer questions about bus, train and school timetables.
Use a calendar to count days and weeks and to find dates before and after a given day.
Solve real-world word problems involving time, checking that your answer makes sense.

concepts.title: The 12-Hour Clock – a.m. and p.m.
concepts.explanation: You already know the 12-hour clock. It is the clock on the wall that counts from 1 to 12. The trouble is that a day has 24 hours, but this clock only shows 12 numbers – so every number comes around twice each day. There is a 7 o'clock in the morning and a 7 o'clock in the evening. How do we tell them apart? We use the little letters a.m. and p.m. a.m. is short for *ante meridiem*, which means 'before midday'. It covers the hours from midnight (12:00 a.m.) up to just before noon (11:59 a.m.). This is the morning…
concepts.example: Here are some times written the 12-hour way, so you can hear how each one sounds:

concepts.title: The 24-Hour Clock
concepts.explanation: The 24-hour clock solves the problem of numbers coming around twice. Instead of starting again at 1 after midday, it keeps counting: 13, 14, 15, all the way up to 23, and then back to 00 at midnight. Because every hour of the day has its own special number, you never need a.m. or p.m. at all. This is why timetables, phones, and airports so often use the 24-hour clock – it can never be confused. The 24-hour clock always uses four digits, with two for the hour and two for the minutes, like 07:30 or 17:45. Times run…
concepts.example: Here are the same times as before, now written the 24-hour way:

concepts.title: Converting Between 12-Hour and 24-Hour Time
concepts.explanation: Being able to switch between the two clocks is one of the most useful skills in this unit. Let us take it one direction at a time. For a.m. times: keep the same hour, but write it with four digits. So 8:15 a.m. becomes 08:15. The one special case is 12:00 a.m. (midnight), which becomes 00:00.
concepts.example: For p.m. times: add 12 to the hour. So 5:45 p.m. becomes 5 + 12 = 17:45. The one special case is 12:00 p.m. (noon), which stays as 12:00 (you do not add 12).

concepts.title: Calculating Time Intervals (How Long Something Lasts)
concepts.explanation: A time interval, also called a duration, is the amount of time that passes between a start time and an end time. If Amina starts her homework at 4:00 p.m. and finishes at 5:30 p.m., the interval is 1 hour and 30 minutes. Working out intervals is a skill you will use every day, so let us build it up carefully, from easy to harder. When the start and end times are in the same hour, you only need to subtract the minutes. To find the interval between 3:15 p.m. and 3:45 p.m., take 45 − 15 = 30 minutes. That is all…
concepts.example: When the times are in different hours, the safest way is to count on in two jumps: first jump whole hours, then jump the extra minutes. Let us find the interval between 2:30 p.m. and 4:15 p.m.

concepts.title: Finding Start Times and End Times
concepts.explanation: Sometimes you know how long something lasts and need to find when it ends or when it began. There are two moves: counting on and counting back. If you know the start time and the duration, count on. Suppose Sahra begins reading at 4:15 p.m. and reads for 1 hour and 45 minutes. Add the hour first: 4:15 p.m. + 1 hour = 5:15 p.m. Then add the minutes: 5:15 p.m. + 45 minutes = 6:00 p.m. So she finishes at 6 o'clock. Adding the hours before the minutes keeps the working tidy.
concepts.example: If you know the end time and the duration, count back. Suppose a party must end at 4:30 p.m. and will last 2 hours and 30 minutes. Take the hours away first: 4:30 p.m. − 2 hours = 2:30 p.m. Then take the minutes away:…

concepts.title: Reading Timetables
concepts.explanation: A timetable is a chart that shows the times when things happen. You meet them everywhere: bus and train timetables show departure and arrival times, school timetables show when lessons begin and end, and TV guides show when programmes start. Timetables almost always use the 24-hour clock, which is one more reason to know it well. Let us read a simple bus timetable for a route in Hargeisa. Each row is one bus journey, and each column is a different stop. You read across a row to follow one bus from stop to stop.
concepts.example: Reading this chart: the first bus leaves Stop A at 07:15 and reaches Stop D at 08:00, so the whole journey takes 45 minutes. If Hassan arrives at Stop B at 08:20, he has just missed the 08:30 bus – wait, no, 08:30 is…

methods.title: How to find how long something lasts
steps: Write down the start time and the end time.
Count on from the start to the next whole hour.
Count on the remaining whole hours, then the extra minutes.
Add all the jumps together to get the duration.

methods.title: How to find an end time
steps: Start at the given time.
Add the whole hours first.
Then add the extra minutes, stepping over the hour if needed.

methods.title: How to find a start time
methods.example: How long is it from 3:15 p.m. to 3:45 p.m.?
steps: Start at the end time.
Take away the whole hours first.
Then take away the extra minutes, stepping back over the hour if needed.

methods.title: An interval across hours
methods.example: A math lesson starts at 10:15 a.m. and ends at 11:00 a.m. How long is it?
steps: Solution: From 10:15 to 11:00, count on the minutes needed to reach the next hour: 15 minutes takes you from 10:15 to 10:30, and 30 more takes you to 11:00.
Altogether that is 45 minutes.
Check the an interval across hours result carefully.

methods.title: Finding an end time
methods.example: Sahra starts her homework at 4:30 p.m. and works for 1 hour and 45 minutes. When does she finish?
steps: Solution: Add the hour first: 4:30 + 1 hour = 5:30 p.m.
Then add the minutes: 5:30 + 45 minutes = 6:15 p.m.
(30 minutes reaches 6:00, and 15 more reaches 6:15).

methods.title: Finding a start time
methods.example: A train journey takes 2 hours and 15 minutes. It arrives at 11:45 a.m. When did it depart?
steps: Solution: Count back.
Take away the hours: 11:45 − 2 hours = 9:45 a.m.
Take away the minutes: 9:45 − 15 minutes = 9:30 a.m.
The train departed at half past nine in the morning.

workedExamples.title: Convert 12-hour to 24-hour
workedExamples.solution: Solution: It is a p.m. time, so add 12 to the hour: 3 + 12 = 15. The minutes stay the same. The answer is 15:30.

workedExamples.title: Convert 24-hour to 12-hour
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The hour is 12 or above, so subtract 12 and add p.m.: 14 − 12 = 2. The answer is 2:05 p.m.

workedExamples.title: An interval in the same hour
workedExamples.prompt: How long is it from 3:15 p.m. to 3:45 p.m.?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The hour does not change, so subtract the minutes: 45 − 15 = 30 minutes.

workedExamples.title: An interval across hours
workedExamples.prompt: A math lesson starts at 10:15 a.m. and ends at 11:00 a.m. How long is it?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: From 10:15 to 11:00, count on the minutes needed to reach the next hour: 15 minutes takes you from 10:15 to 10:30, and 30 more takes you to 11:00. Altogether that is 45 minutes.

workedExamples.title: Finding an end time
workedExamples.prompt: Sahra starts her homework at 4:30 p.m. and works for 1 hour and 45 minutes. When does she finish?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Add the hour first: 4:30 + 1 hour = 5:30 p.m. Then add the minutes: 5:30 + 45 minutes = 6:15 p.m. (30 minutes reaches 6:00, and 15 more reaches 6:15).

workedExamples.title: Finding a start time
workedExamples.prompt: A train journey takes 2 hours and 15 minutes. It arrives at 11:45 a.m. When did it depart?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Count back. Take away the hours: 11:45 − 2 hours = 9:45 a.m. Take away the minutes: 9:45 − 15 minutes = 9:30 a.m. The train departed at half past nine in the morning.

workedExamples.title: A multi-step problem
workedExamples.prompt: A football match starts at 3:30 p.m. The first half lasts 45 minutes, then there is a 15-minute break, then the second half lasts another 45 minutes. When does the match end?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Add each part in order. 3:30 + 45 minutes = 4:15 p.m. (end of first half). 4:15 + 15 minutes = 4:30 p.m. (end of break). 4:30 + 45 minutes = 5:15 p.m. (end of match).

workedExamples.title: A calendar problem
workedExamples.prompt: Today is the 8th. Eid is on the 15th of the same month. How many days must Khadija wait?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Count on from 8 to 15: 15 − 8 = 7 days, which is exactly one week.

workedExamples.title: A timetable problem
workedExamples.prompt: A bus leaves Hargeisa at 10:30 and reaches Berbera at 12:05. How long is the journey?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Count on across noon. From 10:30 to 11:30 is 1 hour, from 11:30 to 12:00 is 30 minutes, and from 12:00 to 12:05 is 5 minutes. Add the jumps: 1 hour + 30 minutes + 5 minutes = 1 hour and 35 minutes.

workedExamples.title: Convert a departure time
workedExamples.prompt: A train timetable shows a departure at 20:15. Write this in 12-hour time.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The hour is 12 or above, so subtract 12 and add p.m.: 20 − 12 = 8. The train departs at 8:15 p.m., quarter past eight in the evening.

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: Using this school timetable, answer the questions below: 09:00–10:00 Maths; 10:00–11:00 English; 11:00–11:15 Break; 11:15–12:15 Science. a) How long is Maths? b) When does Break start and end? c) How long is Science?
workedExamples.solution: a) 1 hour, b) 11:00 to 11:15, c) 1 hour.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: Omar needs to be at the airport at 10:30 a.m. Travel takes 45 minutes and getting ready takes 30 minutes. When should he wake up?

activities.title: Time Conversion Cards
activities.materials: paper or index cards, a pencil.
steps: Make 10 cards showing times in 12-hour format (like 3:30 p.m.).
Make 10 cards showing times in 24-hour format (like 15:30).
Mix all the cards and place them face down.
Take turns picking a card and converting the time to the other format. Check each other's answers.
Keep the cards – they make a quick game you can replay whenever you want to sharpen your converting.

activities.title: My Daily Timeline
activities.materials: a long strip of paper, a ruler, a pencil.
steps: Draw a straight line and mark the hours of your day from waking to sleeping.
Write on the line when you do each thing: prayers, meals, school, play.
Work out the duration of each activity by counting the interval between its start and end.
Find your longest activity and your shortest activity of the day.

activities.title: Build a Family Timetable
steps: Make a timetable for one whole day with at least six activities.
Write a start time and an end time for each activity in 24-hour format.
Calculate how long each activity lasts.
Check there are no gaps or overlaps, then ask a family member a question about your timetable.

activities.title: Bus Stop Investigation
activities.materials: a real bus or TV timetable, or one you invent.
steps: Choose a starting stop and a destination on the timetable.
Find the departure time and the arrival time for one journey.
Work out the journey duration by counting on from departure to arrival.
Try three different journeys and see which is fastest.

activities.title: Countdown Calendar to Eid
activities.materials: a calendar or a grid you draw yourself.
steps: Mark today's date and mark a special day ahead, such as Eid or a birthday.
Count the number of days in between by jumping across the calendar boxes.
Work out how many complete weeks that is, and how many extra days.
Each day, cross off a box and say how many days are left.

activities.title: The Time Problem Swap
steps: Write five time problems of your own – some about intervals, some about start or end times.
Swap your problems with a partner.
Solve each other's problems, showing your working in steps.
Check the answers together and talk about any that were tricky.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand the 24-hour clock or time intervals best? What was the trickiest part? What would you like more practice with? Share your answer with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: 12-Hour to 24-Hour Rule
rules.text: a.m. times: keep the hour, write four digits (8:15 a.m. becomes 08:15). p.m. times: add 12 to the hour (5:45 p.m. becomes 17:45). Special cases: 12 a.m. (midnight) becomes 00:00; 12 p.m. (noon) stays 12:00.

rules.title: 24-Hour to 12-Hour Rule
rules.text: Hours 00 to 11: keep the hour and add a.m. (00:00 becomes 12:00 a.m.). Hours 12 to 23: subtract 12 and add p.m. (17:45 becomes 5:45 p.m.). Special case: 12:00 stays 12:00 p.m. (noon).

rules.title: Time Interval Rule
rules.text: There are 60 minutes in an hour, never 100. Count on in jumps: whole hours first, then the extra minutes. Draw a timeline when a time crosses noon or midnight.

terms: Uses 00:00 to 23:59, no a.m. or p.m.
A chart of when events happen
A chart of days, weeks and months
Another word for how long something takes
commonMistakes: 12 a.m. is midnight (00:00); 12 p.m. is noon
Step to the next hour, then add the rest
Follow one journey along its own row

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Omar needs to be at the airport at 10:30 a.m. Travel takes 45 minutes and getting ready takes 30 minutes. When should he wake up?
A football match starts at 3:30 p.m. Two halves of 45 minutes each are split by a 15-minute break. When does the match end?
A bus leaves Stop A every hour: 07:15, 08:15, 09:15, and so on. Hassan reaches Stop A at 08:40. Which bus can he catch, and how long must he wait?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The 09:15 bus; he waits 35 minutes (from 08:40 to 09:15).

reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 11 days (24th to 30th is 6 days, plus 5 days =

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Finding Start Times and End Times.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Sometimes you know how long something lasts and need to find when it ends or when it began. There are two moves: counting on and counting back. If you know the start time and the duration, count on. Suppose Sahra begins reading at 4:15 p.m. and reads for 1 hour and 45 minutes. Add the hour first: 4:15 p.m. + 1 hour = 5:15 p.m. Then add the minutes: 5:15 p.m. + 45 minutes = 6:00 p.m. So she finishes at 6 o'clock. Adding the hours before the minutes keeps the working tidy.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Reading Timetables.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A timetable is a chart that shows the times when things happen. You meet them everywhere: bus and train timetables show departure and arrival times, school timetables show when lessons begin and end, and TV guides show when programmes start. Timetables almost always use the 24-hour clock, which is one more reason to know it well. Let us read a simple bus timetable for a route in Hargeisa. Each row is one bus journey, and each column is a different stop. You read across a row to follow one bus from stop to stop.

selfAssessment: I can read and write times using both the 12-hour clock (with a.m. and p.m.) and the 24-hour clock.
I can convert a time from 12-hour to 24-hour format and back again.
I can work out a time interval (how long something lasts) between a start time and an end time.
I can find an end time when you know the start time and how long something takes.
I can find a start time when you know the end time and how long something takes.
I can read and answer questions about bus, train and school timetables.
I can use a calendar to count days and weeks and to find dates before and after a given day.
I can solve real-world word problems involving time, checking that your answer makes sense.
